comments:
This paper is a concise overview/survey of embedding models for knowledge base completion. It surveys a broad set of models (TransE/TransH/TransR/TransD and many later variants, bilinear and tensor approaches such as RESCAL/DISTMULT/ComplEx/NTN, path-based methods and PRA-style methods), summarizes common learning objectives and optimization algorithms, and reports up-to-date experimental results on the standard evaluation tasks (entity prediction/link prediction and triple classification) and datasets (WN18, FB15k, WN11, FB13). Strengths:
- Useful, compact consolidation of many influential embedding models and variants in one place.
- Presents experimental comparisons and remarks on initialization effects (e.g., using pre-trained word vectors).
- Covers path-based models and random-walk/PRA approaches in addition to embedding-only methods.
Weaknesses and suggestions for improvement:
- Originality is limited (expected for a survey). The paper would be more valuable if it contained deeper critical analysis of why particular model families succeed/fail under different conditions (e.g., relation types, data sparsity, many-to-many relations) rather than primarily enumerating models.
- The current text (as provided) contains repeated and duplicated fragments and some formatting issues; careful proofreading and cleanup are needed to improve readability.
- More discussion of evaluation protocol choices and their implications would strengthen the survey. For example, explicit commentary on the choice of metrics (mean rank, MRR, Hits@k), dataset construction effects on reported numbers, and best practices for fair comparison (hyperparameter search, initialization, and training details) would help readers interpret the tables.
- The survey could better highlight open problems and future directions (scalability, interpretability, incorporation of textual/contextual data, benchmarks beyond the cited datasets) and provide actionable guidance on when to choose one model family over another.
- Providing clearer, self-contained tables (with consistent columns, dimensions, and a legend) and links to code or reproduced experimental settings would greatly increase reproducibility and utility.
Overall recommendation: the paper is a solid, serviceable overview that will be useful to readers seeking a brief introduction and pointers to the literature. With cleanup and a bit more critical synthesis (and clearer presentation of experimental comparisons), it would be more impactful.
